EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted
Security group allow_ssh_from_world: ingress allows 0.0.0.0/0 on SSH (22) or RDP (3389). Entire internet can attempt auth against management ports.
EC2 instance ec2: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.
Lambda environment variables should not contain secrets: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: environment
Lambda function should have tracing (X-Ray) enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: tracing_config.mode
Lambda function test_lambda: no dead_letter_config. Async-invocation failures retry twice and disappear silently — no signal, no audit trail.
Lambda function test_lambda: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
Lambda function test_lambda: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.

SageMaker notebook instance privesc-sagemakerNotebook: no subnet_id declared. The notebook runs in the AWS-managed network; egress to the public internet is unconstrained, and your VPC SGs / NACLs don't apply.
Long-running Lambda has no DLQ and no reserved concurrency: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: timeout
Lambda integration timeout shape (timeout config sanity): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: timeout
Long-running Lambda with low memory — likely workload misfit: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: timeout

Lambda test_lambda: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.

Lambda test_lambda: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.

Lambda test_lambda: X-Ray tracing is Active but no aws_xray_sampling_rule references this function. At default sampling, high-volume functions produce more trace data than is useful AND bill more than expected.

Lambda test_lambda: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.

Toxic combination on role privesc-high-priv-service-role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. Neither the trust policy nor the permission policy is flagged in isolation — the risk is the path between them.

EC2 instance ec2: IMDSv2 is not enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens ≠ "required") and the attached instance profile role `privesc-high-priv-service-role` grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). Any server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the running application can steal the role credentials in one unauthenticated HTTP request to 169.254.169.254, then immediately use them to full administrator wildcard (Action: "*").

SageMaker notebook `privesc-sagemakerNotebook` runs with role `privesc-sagemaker-role` that grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). Any principal with sagemaker:CreatePresignedNotebookInstanceUrl can open the Jupyter interface and execute arbitrary Python — achieving privilege escalation without direct IAM access.

Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 6.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.

Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_instance) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$4/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.

`aws_instance.ec2` has a public IP and an IAM instance profile, the plan defines admin-equivalent IAM policy/policies (privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy, privesc-high-priv-service-policy, privesc-sre-admin-policy, fp1-allow-and-deny, fp2-allow-all, privesc-high-priv-glue-policy, privesc-high-priv-lambda-policy2, privesc-high-priv-sagemaker-policy), and there is NO WAF web ACL. An SSRF or RCE on this internet-reachable host reaches the instance metadata service, steals the admin-grade role credentials, and pivots to full account control — with no WAF in front to blunt the initial exploit. Each fact is individually common; together they form a complete breach chain.

fullDescription.text: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_instance) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$4/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.

shortDescription.text: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: root_block_device.encrypted

shortDescription.text: [Needs Review] EC2 instance should have EBS root volume encryption enabled

fullDescription.text: Security group allow_ssh_from_world: ingress allows 0.0.0.0/0 on SSH (22) or RDP (3389). Entire internet can attempt auth against management ports.

shortDescription.text: Security group should not allow 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on SSH (port 22) or RDP (port 3389)

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance ec2: no metadata_options block declared. AWS defaults to http_tokens = "optional" (IMDSv1 enabled). SSRF-based credential theft via the instance metadata service remains possible.

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance has no metadata_options block — IMDSv2 not configured

fullDescription.text: `aws_instance.ec2` has a public IP and an IAM instance profile, the plan defines admin-equivalent IAM policy/policies (privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy, privesc-high-priv-service-policy, privesc-sre-admin-policy, fp1-allow-and-deny, fp2-allow-all, privesc-high-priv-glue-policy, privesc-high-priv-lambda-policy2, privesc-high-priv-sagemaker-policy), and there is NO WAF web ACL. An SSRF or RCE on this internet-reachable host reaches the instance metadata service, steals the admin-grade role credentials, and pivots to full account control — with no WAF in front to blunt the initial exploit. Each fact is individually common; together they form a complete breach chain.

shortDescription.text: Public-facing `aws_instance.ec2` + admin IAM + no WAF — account-takeover path

fullDescription.text: IAM access key resource privesc-AssumeRole-start-user is declared in IaC. Confirm there is a rotation process (manual/automated) — CIS requires rotation every 90 days.

shortDescription.text: IAM access keys should be rotated (hardcoded key in source)

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy: enables privilege escalation — full administrator wildcard (Action: "*") — granted on an unconstrained resource (Resource: "*") with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can escalate to broader or administrator privileges.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy enables privilege escalation

fullDescription.text: Toxic combination on role privesc-high-priv-service-role: it is assumable by a compute service (EC2 / ECS / Lambda), AND its attached policies grant full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). An attacker who compromises that workload (SSRF, RCE, dependency takeover) can steal the instance/task role credentials and obtain those permissions. Neither the trust policy nor the permission policy is flagged in isolation — the risk is the path between them.

shortDescription.text: IAM role reachable from a broad principal can escalate privileges

fullDescription.text: EC2 instance ec2: IMDSv2 is not enforced (metadata_options.http_tokens ≠ "required") and the attached instance profile role `privesc-high-priv-service-role` grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). Any server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the running application can steal the role credentials in one unauthenticated HTTP request to 169.254.169.254, then immediately use them to full administrator wildcard (Action: "*").

shortDescription.text: EC2 instance with IMDSv1 enabled and a privileged role enables SSRF → credential theft

fullDescription.text: SageMaker notebook `privesc-sagemakerNotebook` runs with role `privesc-sagemaker-role` that grants full administrator wildcard (Action: "*"). Any principal with sagemaker:CreatePresignedNotebookInstanceUrl can open the Jupyter interface and execute arbitrary Python — achieving privilege escalation without direct IAM access.

shortDescription.text: SageMaker notebook instance has an escalation-capable execution role

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy: grants data exfiltration actions (s3:GetObject, dynamodb:Scan, dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:BatchGetItem, dynamodb:Query, kinesis:GetRecords, sqs:ReceiveMessage, rds:DownloadDBLogFilePortion, athena:GetQueryResults, glue:GetSecurityConfiguration) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can bulk-read or export data across all accessible resources of that service — without needing to escalate privileges first.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants bulk data exfiltration capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy: grants credential extraction actions (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, secretsmanager:BatchGetSecretValue, ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, ssm:GetParametersByPath, kms:Decrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey, sts:GetSessionToken, sts:GetFederationToken) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can read secrets, SSM parameters, or decrypt KMS-protected data across all resources in the account.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants credential/secret extraction capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy: grants resource-exposure actions (s3:PutBucketPolicy, s3:PutBucketAcl, s3:PutObjectAcl, lambda:AddPermission, lambda:AddLayerVersionPermission, sqs:AddPermission, sns:AddPermission, secretsmanager:PutResourcePolicy, kms:PutKeyPolicy, ecr:SetRepositoryPolicy) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can modify the access policy of S3 buckets, Lambda functions, SQS queues, KMS keys, or other resources — potentially making them publicly accessible.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants resource permission modification capability

fullDescription.text: IAM policy privesc-AssumeRole-high-priv-policy: grants infrastructure-modification actions (ec2:RunInstances, ec2:RequestSpotInstances, ecs:RunTask, eks:CreateCluster, rds:CreateDBInstance, rds:RestoreDBInstanceFromDBSnapshot, ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress, ec2:CreateVpc, ec2:CreateKeyPair) on Resource: "*" with no Condition. Any principal holding this policy can launch EC2 instances, run ECS tasks, or modify network rules without restriction — a common cryptomining and lateral-movement vector.

shortDescription.text: IAM policy grants unscoped infrastructure launch/modification capability
